nativeRange.habitat: Rocky littoral zone of Lake Tanganyika; inhabits caves and crevices among rocks at depths up to ~125 m, though most commonly encountered shallower in the rocky coastal belt

decorPreferences: rocky caves and crevices for shelter and nocturnal retreats
sandy or fine-gravel substrate between rocks
dim lighting or shaded areas to accommodate nocturnal nature
secure tank lid (active swimmer at night)

diet.notes: Primarily carnivorous in practice — accepts frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp, mysis, and other meaty invertebrate foods; high-quality sinking carnivore pellets and wafers also taken. Unlike many Synodontis species, significant vegetable matter is generally refused. Wild fish feed on algae scraped from rocks and small benthic invertebrates. Feed at lights-out to ensure adequate intake due to strictly nocturnal activity.

goodWith: medium to large Tanganyikan cichlids (Tropheus, Cyphotilapia, Altolamprologus)
other robust Lake Tanganyika fish of similar size
large rainbowfish in a Lake Tanganyika biotope
other Synodontis of comparable size (with adequate space and caves)
avoidWith: small fish that may be consumed at night (tetras, small rasboras, nano species)
timid or slow-moving bottom dwellers competing for the same caves

imageCandidates.notes: Standard FishBase species image; confirm availability and photographer attribution before use.

summary: Poll’s Synodontis is a robust, heavily-spotted catfish endemic to Lake Tanganyika. Reaching ~12–15 cm, it demands hard alkaline water (pH 7.5–9.0), rocky cave decor, and nocturnal feeding. Produces audible stridulation sounds from pectoral-fin spines. Not yet bred in captivity.
careNotes:
Poll’s Synodontis (Synodontis polli) is an endemic Mochokid catfish from the hard, alkaline waters of Lake Tanganyika, where it inhabits the rocky littoral zone. In the aquarium it demands the chemistry of its home lake: maintain pH 7.5–9.0, high general hardness (gH 8–20), and stable alkalinity; soft or acidic water is wholly unsuitable and will cause rapid decline. Temperature 24–28°C is appropriate for the species.

A tank of at least 200 L with a 120 cm footprint is recommended to provide adequate territory and swimming space. Decor should replicate the rocky habitat: stacked flat stones, slate caves, and crevices are essential for security; this species is secretive during the day and emerges primarily after lights-out. A tight-fitting lid is strongly advised as Poll’s Synodontis can be active at the surface at night.

This species is strictly nocturnal and should be fed at lights-out to ensure it competes successfully for food. Meaty sinking foods (frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp, mysis, carnivore pellets) are readily accepted; unlike many congeners, vegetable-based foods are largely refused. Do not leave food to foul the substrate — water quality must be maintained with efficient filtration and regular water changes, as high nitrates are poorly tolerated in the hard-water Lake Tanganyika biotope.

Poll’s Synodontis produces audible stridulation sounds by rotating the base of the hardened pectoral-fin spine in its socket — a characteristic of the Mochokidae family known colloquially as “squeakers.” This sound is most commonly heard when the fish is startled or handled.

This species is copper-sensitive: avoid copper-based medications (commonly used to treat ich/white spot) in any tank housing this catfish. Use alternative treatments if disease arises.

Semi-aggressive and territorial toward its own kind, it can be kept singly or in small groups if sufficient caves and visual breaks are provided. Compatible with robust Lake Tanganyika cichlids of comparable size. Small tankmates risk predation at night. Rarely available in the trade and sometimes misidentified as the similar but smaller S. petricola; S. polli is distinguished by its larger, irregularly shaped body spots.
breedingNotes: Not recorded in captivity. In the wild, pairs exhibit a distinct pairing behaviour during spawning and the species is oviparous (egg-scattering). Spawning in Lake Tanganyika is thought to be linked to seasonal flooding (approximately July–October). No aquarium breeding protocol is established; spawning attempts have not been documented in hobbyist literature.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Synodontis polli
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (10 cm SL / up to 15 cm wild), temperature 75–82°F (24–28°C), pH 7.5–8.5, hardness 15–35°H, minimum tank 90 × 30 × 30 cm (80 L), diet preferences (meaty foods; vegetable matter largely refused), temperament, native range (Lake Tanganyika; possibly Lake Mweru), depth range (30–125 m), and breeding status (not recorded in captivity).

additional.name: FishBase — Synodontis polli
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 14.8 cm TL), temperature range (22–26°C), native range (Lake Tanganyika, Africa), habitat (benthopelagic freshwater), taxonomy (Mochokidae, Siluriformes), trophic level 2.9 (omnivore), species named after Dr. Max Poll. Confirmed oviparous with distinct pairing during breeding.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Synodontis polli
additional.notes: Supplementary source. Provided habitat detail (rocky littoral, depths to 20 m), Lake Tanganyika water chemistry (pH 8.5–9, dH 4–15, temp 22–26°C), diet (algae scraped from rocks and small invertebrates), spawning season (July–October), coloration (olive-brown with large irregular black spots), and IUCN status (Least Concern). Also noted species described by Gosse 1982.

additional.name: FishBase — Mochokidae family page
additional.notes: Confirmed family classification (Mochokidae = squeakers / upside-down catfishes) and order (Siluriformes). Used to corroborate stridulation trait common to the family.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive spawning recorded. Wild fish form distinct pairs and scatter eggs; spawning season linked to seasonal flooding (July–October in Lake Tanganyika). No established aquarium protocol exists.
